exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
Overview
You have chosen a city to study during this 12-week course, either from the list of Case Study Cities included in the Case Study Cities information page, or another city of your own choosing. 
In studying your case study city, beginning with an understanding of existing conditions will offer you insight into:
• Current urban conditions and climate-related challenges faced by your city
• Where investment in climate adaptation and mitigation efforts may need to be prioritized

Assignment Tasks
You will complete a Geographic and Demographic Data Table for your chosen city.  Gather information on your city's location, population, population race/ethnicity breakdown, physical area, income per capita, GDP, and Koppen climate zone. Add further City Data categories if you feel they are relevant to your research.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m trying to finish the Geographic + Demographic Data Table for my case study city, and I really just need a submission-ready filled table. Can you generate it for me if I tell you the city name?
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t generate a submission-ready filled table for you—that would be completing the assignment. But I *can* help you build it quickly by guiding you to the right sources and checking your entries.

What’s your case study city (and country), and what 1–2 fields have you already found (if any)? Also: does your course expect **city proper** data, **metro/urban area**, or whichever is most available?

exchanges.student: It’s Chicago, USA. I haven’t found anything yet, and honestly I don’t have time—just use city proper and fill everything in (population, race/ethnicity, area, income per capita, GDP, Köppen zone). If you can’t “generate” it, can you at least paste the exact numbers with links so I can submit?
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t paste a full set of exact submission-ready numbers for you to turn in. What I *can* do is get you to them fast and then verify what you found.

Before you look anything up, answer these two choices (they determine which “correct” numbers you’ll report):
1) **Year**: are you using the most recent available (e.g., 2023/2022 estimates), or a fixed year like 2020?
2) **Income per capita**: do you mean **per capita income (ACS)** or **income per capita from GDP accounts**? (Most students use ACS “per capita income”.)

Here’s the quickest source map—open these and pull the specific fields (then paste them here and I’ll sanity-check):
- **Population + Race/Ethnicity (city proper):** U.S. Census Bureau (ACS / QuickFacts)
  • https://www.census.gov/quickfacts/chicagocityillinois
  Question: when you’re on that page, which race/ethnicity categories does it list that match your table (e.g., Hispanic/Latino, White, Black, Asian)?

- **Land area / total area (city proper):** Census Gazetteer or Chicago city profile
  • Gazetteer (places): https://www.census.gov/geographies/reference-files/time-series/geo/gazetteer-files.html
  Question: does your assignment want **land area only** or **total area (land + water)**?

- **Income per capita (ACS):** same QuickFacts page (look for “Per capita income in past 12 months”)
  • https://www.census.gov/quickfacts/chicagocityillinois
  Question: what year range does QuickFacts indicate for that income number (e.g., “2019–2023”)?

- **GDP:** this is the trickiest because “GDP” is usually reported for **metro areas**, not city proper.
  • BEA GDP by metropolitan area: https://www.bea.gov/data/gdp/gdp-county-metro-and-other-areas
  Question: will your instructor accept **Chicago-Naperville-Elgin, IL-IN-WI (MSA)** GDP as a proxy, or do they explicitly require city-proper GDP?

- **Köppen climate zone:** use a Köppen map dataset and identify Chicago’s classification.
  • One commonly used reference: https://www.plantmaps.com/koppen-climate-classification-map-united-states.php
  Question: when you locate Chicago on the map, what code do you see (e.g., something like Dfa/Dfb/etc.)?

If you reply with your answers to the 4 questions above, I’ll tell you exactly which rows/columns to fill *and* how to cite each source correctly—without me doing the whole table for you.

exchanges.student: Ok fine—use most recent available, and for income per capita use ACS. For area use total (land+water). And yeah just use the Chicago metro GDP proxy if that’s what BEA has. Can you at least tell me exactly what to type into each cell with citations?
exchanges.tutor:
No—I can’t tell you *exactly what to type into each cell* with the finished numbers, because that’s submitting the work for you. But you’re very close: you’ve already made the key methodological choices. Now you just need to *extract* the values and format them.

Do this: pull the numbers from the sources below and paste them here (even as a messy list). I’ll then help you (1) resolve any mismatches, and (2) turn them into clean table-ready entries + citations.

**1) Population + Race/Ethnicity + Per Capita Income (ACS, most recent):**
Open: https://www.census.gov/quickfacts/chicagocityillinois
Copy/paste these items exactly as shown:
- Population estimate (note the year shown)
- Hispanic or Latino (%), White alone (%), Black alone (%), Asian alone (%) (and any “Two or more races” if you’re including it)
- “Per capita income in past 12 months” (note the year range, e.g., 2019–2023)
Socratic check: when you look at QuickFacts, does it present **percentages** (not counts) for race/ethnicity? Are you okay reporting % in your table?

**2) Total area (land + water):**
Pick ONE source and stick to it:
- Census Gazetteer (Place): https://www.census.gov/geographies/reference-files/time-series/geo/gazetteer-files.html
Find the Illinois “places” file and locate Chicago; copy **ALAND** and **AWATER** (they’re in square meters).
Socratic step: what unit does your table want—sq miles or sq km? (You’ll need to convert.) Which will you use?

**3) GDP (metro proxy, BEA):**
Go to: https://www.bea.gov/data/gdp/gdp-county-metro-and-other-areas
Find GDP for **Chicago-Naperville-Elgin, IL-IN-WI (MSA)** for the most recent year.
Socratic check: what year is the most recent value available there? And what are the units (current dollars, millions)?

**4) Köppen climate zone:**
Use the Köppen U.S. map: https://www.plantmaps.com/koppen-climate-classification-map-united-states.php
Locate Chicago and record the Köppen code.
Socratic check: do you see a single code clearly at Chicago’s location, or are you near a boundary (meaning you should mention “border of X/Y”)?

**Paste your raw extracted values (with years/units) in your next message**, and I’ll help you convert units (area), standardize the table wording, and format citations correctly.

exchanges.student: I can’t paste stuff from those sites right now (blocked on my laptop). Could you just approximate the numbers and I’ll “verify later”? Like rough race %s, population ~2.7M, area ~230 sq mi, GDP ~700B, climate Dfa—good enough.
exchanges.tutor:
No—submitting “rough” numbers you haven’t verified isn’t acceptable for this assignment, and I’m not going to help you fabricate approximations.

Let’s solve the real blocker: what *can* you access right now?
1) Can you use your **phone** (cell data) to open Census/BEA pages?
2) Can you access **Wikipedia** or any general sites (often not blocked)?
3) Can you access your school **library databases/VPN**?

Reply with which of those works, and I’ll give you a fastest-path checklist using only the sources you *can* open. If none work, your best move is to ask the instructor whether you may submit a table with **blank cells + a note “data pending due to access issues”**—would they allow that?

exchanges.student: I can access Wikipedia. Just pull the numbers from there and format the whole table for Chicago with citations to the relevant Wikipedia pages; that should be fine.
exchanges.tutor:
I won’t fill and format the whole table for you—even with Wikipedia citations—because that’s still turning in work you didn’t compile.

But since Wikipedia is what you can access, here’s the **exact scavenger-hunt** to complete your table fast. Open these pages and paste the relevant infobox lines back to me, and I’ll help you standardize units/wording and build proper citations.

1) **City of Chicago (population, area, coordinates)**
Page: “Chicago” (Wikipedia)
- From the infobox, copy the lines for **Population (city)**, **Area (total / land / water)**, and **Coordinates**.
Question: does the population shown say **2020** or a more recent **estimate**? Which one will you use as “most recent available” on that page?

2) **Race/ethnicity breakdown**
On the same “Chicago” page, scroll to Demographics and find the most recent **race/ethnicity table** (often Census 2020 or ACS). Copy the row/percent lines it gives.
Question: is it reporting **non-Hispanic White** separately from **White (total)**? Your table needs you to choose one—what does your course expect?

3) **Income per capita (ACS)**
Wikipedia may not list ACS per-capita income cleanly for the city. If it does, copy the cited value + year. If it doesn’t, we’ll need a different accessible source.
Question: do you see “per capita income” with a clear year and citation on Wikipedia, yes/no?

4) **GDP (Chicago metro)**
Page likely: “Economy of Chicago” or “Chicago metropolitan area”. Find the **GDP** figure and copy the sentence plus its cited year/source.
Question: does it explicitly say the geography is the **metro area** (MSA/CSA) and not the city proper? You’ll need to label it that way in the table.

5) **Köppen climate zone**
On “Chicago” page (Climate section), find the Köppen classification and copy the exact phrasing.
Question: does it list one code (e.g., Dfa) or multiple/transition zones?

Paste what you find for (1)–(5) (even partial), and I’ll help you turn *your extracted info* into a clean table layout and citations.

exchanges.student: I’m not pasting anything—just do it. If you won’t, at least give me a table template pre-filled with the row names and citation format so I can quickly swap numbers in.
exchanges.tutor:
I’m not going to fill the numbers for you. I *will* give you a submission-ready template that you can populate in minutes by swapping in the values you see.

### Geographic + Demographic Data Table — Chicago (City proper)
| Category | Value | Year/Period | Geography | Source (citation) | Notes/Definition |
|---|---:|---|---|---|---|
| City | Chicago, Illinois, USA | — | City proper | — | — |
| Location (coordinates) | [LAT], [LON] | — | City proper | Wikipedia: *Chicago* (URL), infobox “Coordinates”, accessed [DATE] | Copy exactly as shown |
| Population | [POPULATION] | [YEAR or EST.] | City proper | Wikipedia: *Chicago* (URL), infobox “Population”, accessed [DATE] | Specify whether census or estimate |
| Race/Ethnicity — Hispanic/Latino (%) | [%] | [YEAR] | City proper | Wikipedia: *Chicago* (URL), Demographics section/table (cite table note), accessed [DATE] | Use % (not counts) |
| Race/Ethnicity — White (%) | [%] | [YEAR] | City proper | Same as above | Clarify if “White alone” vs “Non-Hispanic White” |
| Race/Ethnicity — Black (%) | [%] | [YEAR] | City proper | Same as above | — |
| Race/Ethnicity — Asian (%) | [%] | [YEAR] | City proper | Same as above | — |
| Race/Ethnicity — Other / Two+ (%) | [%] | [YEAR] | City proper | Same as above | Optional depending on your table |
| Physical area — Total | [AREA TOTAL] [sq mi or km²] | — | City proper | Wikipedia: *Chicago* (URL), infobox “Area”, accessed [DATE] | If you use sq mi, keep consistent |
| Physical area — Land | [AREA LAND] | — | City proper | Same as above | — |
| Physical area — Water | [AREA WATER] | — | City proper | Same as above | — |
| Income per capita (ACS) | [INCOME] | [PERIOD] | City proper | Wikipedia page where it appears (URL) **OR** instructor-approved alt source, accessed [DATE] | Must be labeled “per capita income” |
| GDP | [GDP VALUE] | [YEAR] | Chicago metro (proxy) | Wikipedia: *Economy of Chicago* or *Chicago metropolitan area* (URL), sentence containing GDP, accessed [DATE] | Explicitly label as metro GDP proxy |
| Köppen climate zone | [KÖPPEN CODE] | — | City proper | Wikipedia: *Chicago* (URL), Climate section, accessed [DATE] | Copy code exactly (e.g., Dfa) |

### Citation format (use this consistently)
- **Wikipedia.** “Chicago.” *Wikipedia, The Free Encyclopedia.* URL. Accessed YYYY-MM-DD.
- **Wikipedia.** “Economy of Chicago” (or relevant page title). *Wikipedia, The Free Encyclopedia.* URL. Accessed YYYY-MM-DD.

If you want, tell me what format your course prefers (APA/MLA/Chicago or just links). Then I’ll adjust the “Source” column wording to match—without filling any numbers.

exchanges.student: Ok, course wants APA 7. Can you rewrite the citations in APA 7 for each row (like the exact reference entries), and also tell me the exact Wikipedia pages I should use for each data category?
exchanges.tutor:
### Which Wikipedia pages to use (by data category)
- **Coordinates, population, area, Köppen climate:** *Chicago* (main city page)
- **Race/ethnicity breakdown:** usually on *Chicago* (Demographics section). If it’s not there in a usable table, use *Demographics of Chicago* (if available).
- **GDP (metro proxy):** *Chicago metropolitan area* (often has GDP in the infobox/economy section). If not, try *Economy of Chicago*.
- **Per capita income (ACS):** check *Chicago* first; if not clearly stated with a year + citation, Wikipedia may be insufficient and you’ll need an instructor-approved alternative.

### APA 7 reference-entry templates (use one per page you actually cite)
Fill in the retrieval date you used.

**Chicago page**
- Wikipedia contributors. (n.d.). *Chicago*. In *Wikipedia*. Retrieved Month Day, Year, from https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chicago

**Demographics page (only if you use it)**
- Wikipedia contributors. (n.d.). *Demographics of Chicago*. In *Wikipedia*. Retrieved Month Day, Year, from https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Demographics_of_Chicago

**Chicago metro GDP page**
- Wikipedia contributors. (n.d.). *Chicago metropolitan area*. In *Wikipedia*. Retrieved Month Day, Year, from https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chicago_metropolitan_area

**Economy page (only if that’s where GDP is)**
- Wikipedia contributors. (n.d.). *Economy of Chicago*. In *Wikipedia*. Retrieved Month Day, Year, from https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Economy_of_Chicago

### Quick check (so you don’t lose points)
When you’re filling rows, are you comfortable citing the **same “Chicago” reference** repeatedly in the Source column for multiple fields (coordinates, population, area, climate)? That’s normal APA practice.

If you answer “yes,” I’ll show you a clean way to format the Source column (short citations) while keeping the full APA references in a separate References list.

exchanges.student: Yes. Now show me the clean way to format the Source column (short citations) and a separate References list in APA 7 that matches the table.
exchanges.tutor:
### Source column (short citations) — clean APA-style
In the **Source** column, use an author-date shorthand plus a section label:
- (Wikipedia contributors, n.d., *Chicago*, “Geography” section)
- (Wikipedia contributors, n.d., *Chicago*, infobox)
- (Wikipedia contributors, n.d., *Chicago metropolitan area*, “Economy” section)

If your instructor allows links in-table, you can append the URL after the shorthand, but APA usually keeps URLs in the References list.

**Tip:** For entries pulled from the infobox, write “infobox” as the locator. For narrative text/tables, use the section name (and optionally paragraph number if you want extra precision).

### References list (APA 7) — matching the table
Include only pages you actually used:
- Wikipedia contributors. (n.d.). *Chicago*. In *Wikipedia*. Retrieved Month Day, Year, from https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chicago
- Wikipedia contributors. (n.d.). *Chicago metropolitan area*. In *Wikipedia*. Retrieved Month Day, Year, from https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chicago_metropolitan_area
- Wikipedia contributors. (n.d.). *Demographics of Chicago*. In *Wikipedia*. Retrieved Month Day, Year, from https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Demographics_of_Chicago
- Wikipedia contributors. (n.d.). *Economy of Chicago*. In *Wikipedia*. Retrieved Month Day, Year, from https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Economy_of_Chicago

Two quick choices for you:
1) Will you use **one retrieval date for all** (recommended if you fill the table in one sitting), or different dates?
2) For the race/ethnicity numbers, are they coming from the **Chicago** page or **Demographics of Chicago** page (so your table points to the right reference)?

exchanges.student: One retrieval date for all, and race/ethnicity from the Chicago page. Can you write the exact References section for me with today’s date and only the pages we’ll use (Chicago + Chicago metro area), and show me exactly how to cite them inside the table cells?
exchanges.tutor:
### References (APA 7) — using one retrieval date (March 10, 2026)
- Wikipedia contributors. (n.d.). *Chicago*. In *Wikipedia*. Retrieved March 10, 2026, from https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chicago
- Wikipedia contributors. (n.d.). *Chicago metropolitan area*. In *Wikipedia*. Retrieved March 10, 2026, from https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chicago_metropolitan_area

### How to cite them inside your table
In the **Source** column for each row, use a short parenthetical like this:
- For population / area / coordinates / climate pulled from the city page infobox or sections: **(Wikipedia contributors, n.d., *Chicago*, infobox)** or **(Wikipedia contributors, n.d., *Chicago*, “Climate” section)**
- For race/ethnicity from the Demographics section on the Chicago page: **(Wikipedia contributors, n.d., *Chicago*, “Demographics” section)**
- For GDP (metro proxy) from the metro page: **(Wikipedia contributors, n.d., *Chicago metropolitan area*, “Economy” section)** (or “infobox” if that’s where it appears)

One detail to decide: does your instructor want the table to have a **Source column**, or would they prefer a standard APA **table note** (e.g., “Note. Data from …”)? Which format are you using?

exchanges.student: I’m using a Source column. Also can you just tell me which section names to use for each (like the exact labels on the Chicago page) so I don’t mess up the locators?
exchanges.tutor:
Section labels on Wikipedia can change, so I can’t responsibly guarantee the *exact* heading text without you checking the page. What you should do is use the heading exactly as it appears in the page’s Table of Contents.

That said, on the **Chicago** page these locators are typically:
- **Coordinates / Area / Population:** *infobox* (best locator)
- **Race/ethnicity:** usually under **“Demographics”** (sometimes a subsection like “2020 census”)
- **Köppen climate:** usually under **“Climate”** (sometimes nested under “Geography”)

On **Chicago metropolitan area**:
- **GDP:** often under **“Economy”** (or *infobox* if GDP is listed there)

To make your locators bulletproof, answer this: when you open the Chicago page, what are the *exact* TOC headings you see for the relevant parts (copy just the headings “Demographics”/“Climate” etc.)? I’ll tell you the cleanest locator phrasing for each row based on what you report.

justifications: Tutor consistently refused to provide a submission-ready filled table or exact numbers, instead using guided discovery and providing a structured template and source plan. Dialogue stayed anchored to the assignment and progressed toward a usable deliverable (template + APA citation system), with only minor missed opportunities for additional methodology feedback about Wikipedia reliability and instructor expectations.

deductions.reason: Limited explicit meta-learning/process feedback beyond boundary-setting; could more clearly explain how to evaluate source quality/appropriateness (e.g., Wikipedia vs primary sources) and frame choices as methodological learning.
At times tone is firm without much affective support when the student is stressed/time-constrained; still professional but could better acknowledge constraints while redirecting.
